concepts.title: Standard form
concepts.conceptBody:
A geometric construction of the parabola 

If several lines are drawn, all parallel to the directrix and on the same side of it as the focus, such that the distance from the directrix to each line is not less than a units, it is possible to construct many points of a parabola. In Fig., for example, the several points on the curve are located in the following manner: With $\left| {{\text{A}}_{1}}\text{D} \right|$ as a radius and $\text{F}$ as a center, swing areas intersecting the perpendicular through ${{\text{A}}_{1}}$ at the points ${{\text{P}}_{1}}$ and $\text{P}_{1}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}$, respectively. Perform similar operations with $\left| {{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{D} \right|,\left| {{\text{A}}_{3}}\text{D} \right|$, etc., as radii and $\text{F}$ as a center; and obtain successively the points ${{\text{P}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{P}}_{2}}',{{\text{P}}_{3}}$ and $\text{P}_{3}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}$, etc.. 
 

Let $\text{S}$ be the fixed point and $\text{ZM}$ the directrix. We require therefore, the locus of a point $\text{P}$ which moves so that its distance from $\text{S}$ is always equal to $\text{PM}$, its perpendicular

distance from ZM.

Draw SZ perpendicular to the directrix and bisect SZ in the point A; produce ZA to X.

The point $\text{A}$ is clearly a point on the curve and is called the Vertex of the Parabola.

Take $\text{A}$ as origin, $\text{AX}$ as the axis of $\text{x}$, and $\text{AY}$, perpendicular to it, as the axis of

$\text{y}$. Let the distance $\text{ZA}$, or $\text{AS}$, be called a, and let $\text{P}$ be any point on the curve

whose coordinates are $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$.

Join SP, and draw PN and PM perpendicular respectively to the axis and directrix. We

have the, $\text{S}{{\text{P}}^{2}}=\text{P}{{\text{M}}^{2}}$,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & ~{{(\text{x}-\text{a})}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}=\text{Z}{{\text{N}}^{2}}={{(\text{a}+\text{x})}^{2}}, \\ \therefore & {{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax} \\ \end{array}$ …..(1)

This is also standard form of parabola. 

 

Note : 

The parabola is symmetric with respect to the line through the focus

perpendicular to the directrix, since the image $Q$ of every point $\text{P}$ in

the parabola with respect to this line is also a point on the parabola.
